metadata.description: Statistical analysis supporting the structural bubble comparison

hhi_concentration.interpretation: AI infrastructure capex HHI = 2564, far above the DOJ 'highly concentrated' threshold of 2,500. This means AI investment is concentrated among very few actors, structurally incompatible with the distributed retail participation required for bubble crash dynamics. Estimated dot-com era HHI ~200 (thousands of companies) — AI is 13x more concentrated.

uniform_perturbation.description: Each score randomly perturbed by -0.5, 0, or +0.5

adversarial_25pct.description: 25% chance each feature is scored one category too low

extreme_adversarial_50pct.description: 50% chance each feature is scored one category too low

monte_carlo.interpretation: Under uniform random perturbation (±0.5 per feature), 0.0% of 100,000 simulations reach the bubble threshold of 4.0. Even under adversarial assumptions (25% chance each feature is underscored), only 0.0% reach the threshold. Under extreme adversarial assumptions (50% chance each feature underscored), 0.0% reach the threshold. The conclusion is robust to substantial scoring uncertainty.

forward_pe.interpretation: Forward P/E (18.6) is 32% below trailing P/E (27.2), indicating the market expects earnings growth to justify current valuations — the opposite of speculative disconnect.

pe_distribution.interpretation: AI infrastructure P/E ratios (mean 27.2, CV 18%) cluster tightly around the S&P 500 historical average of 20.0. The mean trailing P/E is 27% of the dot-com peak NASDAQ median (100.0). Forward P/E compression of 32% indicates expected earnings growth, not speculation.

capex_sustainability.interpretation: AI infrastructure companies spend 27% of revenue on capex (range: 2-43%). This is elevated compared to utilities (15-25%) but dramatically below dot-com era companies that spent more than 100% of revenue. Crucially, this capex produces physical assets (data centers, GPUs) with residual value, unlike dot-com marketing spend or WeWork lease subsidies that produce no recoverable assets.

statistical_tests.interpretation: Fisher's exact test yields p = 0.0000, confirming that AI's feature profile differs significantly from historical bubbles (p < 0.05). The effect size is very large (Cohen's d = 10.7). A permutation test (100,000 permutations) confirms the result (p = 0.2005). AI scores 0.5/6.0 vs historical mean 5.62/6.0 — a gap of 5.12 points that is extremely unlikely under the null hypothesis that AI shares the same structural profile as historical bubbles.

correlation.caveat: Small sample size (n=4). Correlation is reported for completeness but should be interpreted cautiously.
correlation.interpretation: Spearman's rho = -0.21 between structural score and peak decline across 4 historical bubbles. While the small sample limits statistical power, the direction is consistent with the framework: higher structural scores are associated with more severe crashes. AI's score of 0.5 would predict minimal crash severity under this relationship.
